questions.questionText: Short announcement. “Good morning. The library will close at 5 p.m. today.” When will the library close?
questions.explanation: The message says “close at 5 p.m.”

questions.questionText: Phone message. “Hi Sam, I’m on the bus. I’ll arrive in ten minutes.” When will the speaker arrive?
questions.explanation: The speaker says “in ten minutes.”

questions.questionText: Teacher reminder. “Please hand in your homework on Friday, not Thursday.” When is the homework due?
questions.explanation: It clearly says “on Friday.”

questions.questionText: Shop sign. “Buy one drink, get one free — today only.” What does the offer mean?

options.B: You get a free drink when you buy one

questions.explanation: “Buy one, get one free” means one free item with one purchase.

questions.questionText: Friend’s text. “Let’s meet at the MTR exit A at 3:30.” Where will they meet?
questions.explanation: The location is “MTR exit A.”

questions.questionText: Weather update. “It will be sunny in the morning, but rainy in the afternoon.” When will it rain?
questions.explanation: It says “rainy in the afternoon.”

questions.questionText: Class announcement. “The English speaking practice is moved to Room 402.” Which room is the practice in now?
questions.explanation: It is “moved to Room 402.”

questions.questionText: Cafeteria notice. “Today’s set lunch includes soup and a main dish.” What is included?
questions.explanation: The notice lists soup + main dish.

questions.questionText: Bus driver. “Next stop: City Hall.” What is the next stop?
questions.explanation: The driver announces “Next stop: City Hall.”

questions.questionText: Lost and found. “A black wallet was found near the playground. Please go to the office.” What was found?
questions.explanation: The message says “black wallet.”

questions.questionText: Simple dialogue. A: “Do you want tea or coffee?” B: “Coffee, please.” What does B want?
questions.explanation: B says “Coffee, please.”

questions.questionText: Cinema info. “The movie starts at 7:15. Please be seated by 7:10.” When should people be seated?
questions.explanation: It says “be seated by 7:10.”

questions.questionText: Email snippet. “Your order will arrive on Tuesday.” When will it arrive?
questions.explanation: “Arrive on Tuesday.”

questions.questionText: School club poster. “Basketball practice: Wednesdays, 4–6 p.m.” Which day is practice?
questions.explanation: The poster says “Wednesdays.”

questions.questionText: Parent message. “Remember to bring your umbrella.” What should the student bring?
questions.explanation: The instruction is “bring your umbrella.”

questions.questionText: Train announcement. “This train is for Tsim Sha Tsui. Please mind the gap.” Where is this train going?
questions.explanation: It says “train is for Tsim Sha Tsui.”

questions.questionText: Classroom instruction. “Work in pairs and finish Questions 1 to 5.” How should students work?
questions.explanation: “Work in pairs.”

questions.questionText: Announcement (multiple details). “Due to a staff meeting, the school office will close at 3:30 today. If you need documents, please collect them before 3:00.” By what time should you collect documents?
questions.explanation: It says “collect them before 3:00.”

questions.questionText: Dialogue (inference). A: “Did you finish the report?” B: “Not yet. I’ll stay up tonight to do it.” What can we infer about B?

options.B: B will work on it tonight

questions.explanation: “Stay up tonight” implies working tonight.

questions.questionText: Customer complaint (main point). “I ordered a medium pizza, but I received a small one. Could you replace it or refund the difference?” What is the problem?
questions.explanation: Medium was ordered; small was received.

questions.questionText: School policy (compare conditions). “Students may use the computer room during lunch only if a teacher is present. After school, students must sign in at the office first.” What must students do to use the computer room after school?

options.B: Sign in at the office first

questions.explanation: After school rule: “must sign in.”

questions.questionText: Public transport notice (cause + effect). “Service on the Tsuen Wan Line is delayed due to a signal fault. Consider using the bus as an alternative.” Why is the service delayed?
questions.explanation: The cause is stated: “signal fault.”

questions.questionText: Dialogue (clarifying a misunderstanding). A: “So we’re meeting on Thursday, right?” B: “No, I said Tuesday. Thursday I have tutorial class.” When are they meeting?
questions.explanation: B corrects it to Tuesday.

questions.questionText: Email (purpose). “I’m writing to confirm our interview appointment at 10:00 a.m. on 12 May. Please reply to acknowledge.” Why did the sender write the email?
questions.explanation: “Writing to confirm” is explicit.

questions.questionText: Announcement (best action). “The fire alarm has been activated. Please leave the building by the nearest exit. Do not use the lift.” What should people do?
questions.explanation: Evacuation instruction: nearest exit; no lift.

questions.questionText: Dialogue (choice with constraints). A: “We can take a taxi, but it’s expensive.” B: “Let’s take the MTR. It’s cheaper and faster at this time.” Why does B prefer the MTR?
questions.explanation: B gives two reasons: cheaper + faster.

questions.questionText: Short talk (identify speaker’s attitude). “I understand your point, but I’m not fully convinced. Could you give one more example?” What is the speaker’s attitude?

options.B: Politely doubts and asks for more support

questions.explanation: “Not fully convinced” + request for example shows polite doubt.

questions.questionText: Lost item notice (key detail). “If you lost a set of keys with a red keychain, please contact Mr. Lee in Room 105.” What is special about the keys?
questions.explanation: The description is “red keychain.”

questions.questionText: Dialogue (sequence/time). A: “The train leaves at 8:05.” B: “Then we should get there by 7:50 to be safe.” What time do they plan to arrive at the station?
questions.explanation: B suggests arriving by 7:50.

questions.questionText: News brief (main idea). “The city will plant 5,000 trees this year to improve air quality and provide more shade in public areas.” What is the main purpose of planting trees?

options.B: To improve air quality and shade

questions.explanation: Two purposes are stated: air quality + shade.

questions.questionText: Classroom discussion summary (identify conclusion). “Most students agreed that homework is useful, but they suggested reducing the amount during exam weeks.” What did most students suggest?
questions.explanation: The suggestion is explicitly stated.

questions.questionText: Service call (pick the best interpretation). “Your internet will be unavailable from 1 a.m. to 3 a.m. for scheduled maintenance.” What does “scheduled maintenance” mean here?

options.B: Planned work to improve or fix the service

questions.explanation: “Scheduled” means planned; maintenance is service work.

questions.questionText: Dialogue (polite refusal). A: “Can you join the meeting at 6?” B: “I’d like to, but I have a part-time job then. Could we do 7 instead?” What does B do?

options.C: Cannot at 6 and suggests a new time

questions.explanation: B can’t do 6, but proposes 7.

questions.questionText: School announcement. “The sports day has been postponed because of heavy rain. It will be held next Friday instead.” Why is sports day postponed?
questions.explanation: It says “because of heavy rain.”

questions.questionText: Dialogue (plans). A: “Let’s meet at 2:00.” B: “I can’t. How about 2:30?” A: “Okay.” What time will they meet?
questions.explanation: They agree on 2:30.

questions.questionText: Customer service message. “To change your password, click ‘Settings’, then ‘Account’, and choose ‘Reset Password’.” What should you click first?
questions.explanation: The first step is “click ‘Settings’.”

questions.questionText: Teacher feedback. “Your presentation was clear, but please speak a little louder next time.” What should the student improve?
questions.explanation: The teacher says “speak a little louder.”

questions.questionText: Restaurant notice. “Lunch set is available until 2:30 p.m. After that, only the regular menu is served.” What happens after 2:30 p.m.?

options.C: Only the regular menu is served

questions.explanation: It states “only the regular menu.”

questions.questionText: Travel announcement. “Passengers to Gate 12, your flight will begin boarding in 15 minutes.” What will happen in 15 minutes?
questions.explanation: “Begin boarding in 15 minutes.”

questions.questionText: Dialogue (choosing). A: “Do you want to watch a comedy or an action movie?” B: “Action, but not too violent.” What does B prefer?
questions.explanation: B chooses action with a condition.

questions.questionText: Club email. “Please arrive at 3:45 to set up. The meeting starts at 4:00 sharp.” Why should members arrive at 3:45?
questions.explanation: It says “arrive…to set up.”

questions.questionText: Phone message (problem + solution). “The lift is out of order today. Please use the stairs or the lift in Block B.” What should people do?

options.B: Use the stairs or Block B lift

questions.explanation: Two alternatives are given.

questions.questionText: Library rule. “You may borrow up to three books for two weeks.” How many books can you borrow?
questions.explanation: “Up to three books.”

questions.questionText: Weather warning. “A strong wind signal is in force. Secure loose objects near windows.” What should people do?
questions.explanation: The instruction is “secure loose objects.”

questions.questionText: Online order update. “Your package was delivered, but no one was at home. It will be re-delivered tomorrow afternoon.” When will it be re-delivered?
questions.explanation: It says “tomorrow afternoon.”

questions.questionText: Dialogue (reason). A: “Why didn’t you join the basketball match?” B: “I had a fever, so I stayed home.” Why didn’t B join?
questions.explanation: B gives the reason: “I had a fever.”

questions.questionText: School trip note. “Bring your student ID and a packed lunch. Do not bring valuables.” What should students NOT bring?
questions.explanation: It explicitly says “Do not bring valuables.”

questions.questionText: Small news report. “The road will be closed for repairs from 10 p.m. to 6 a.m.” When is the road closed?
questions.explanation: The time period is given directly.

questions.questionText: Dialogue (preference change). A: “Do you still want to eat Japanese food?” B: “Actually, I’d rather have Thai food today.” What does B want now?
questions.explanation: “Actually…rather have Thai food.”

questions.questionText: Teacher instruction (sequence). “First, read the article. Then answer Questions 1–8. Finally, check your answers with a partner.” What should students do after answering the questions?
questions.explanation: The final step is “check…with a partner.”

questions.questionText: The word "abundant" means?
questions.explanation: "Abundant" means plentiful or existing in large amounts.

questions.questionText: Hong Kong has a famous harbour. Many tourists visit it every year. What is the main attraction mentioned?
questions.explanation: The passage directly says Hong Kong has a famous harbour.

questions.questionText: The Peak Tram takes people up to Victoria Peak. Where does the Peak Tram go?
questions.explanation: It “takes people up to Victoria Peak.”

questions.questionText: MTR is the fastest way to travel in Hong Kong. It is clean and air-conditioned. What is one good thing about the MTR?
questions.explanation: The text states the MTR is clean (and air-conditioned).

questions.questionText: Dim sum is a traditional Hong Kong food. People eat it with tea in the morning. When do people usually eat dim sum?
questions.explanation: The passage says people eat it in the morning.

questions.questionText: Ocean Park has pandas and dolphins. What animals can you see at Ocean Park?
questions.explanation: The animals mentioned are pandas and dolphins.

questions.questionText: Hong Kong Disneyland is fun for children. Who enjoys Disneyland the most?
questions.explanation: The sentence says it is fun for children.

questions.questionText: The weather in Hong Kong is hot and humid in summer. How is the summer weather?
questions.explanation: It directly states hot and humid.

questions.questionText: Students in Hong Kong study hard for the HKDSE. Why do students study hard?
questions.explanation: They study hard for the HKDSE and want good results.

questions.questionText: The Star Ferry is cheap and has nice views of the harbour. What is one advantage of the Star Ferry?
questions.explanation: One stated advantage is it is cheap.

questions.questionText: Tai O is a fishing village in Hong Kong. It has stilt houses. What is special about Tai O?
questions.explanation: The special feature mentioned is stilt houses.

questions.questionText: English is important for jobs in Hong Kong. Why is English important?
questions.explanation: The passage links English to jobs.

questions.questionText: The Big Buddha on Lantau Island is very big and famous. Where is the Big Buddha?
questions.explanation: It says it is on Lantau Island.

questions.questionText: Reading books helps improve English. What helps improve English?
questions.explanation: The passage states reading books helps.

questions.questionText: Hong Kong has many shopping malls like Times Square. Name one famous shopping place.
questions.explanation: Times Square is named as a shopping mall.

questions.questionText: Typhoons sometimes come to Hong Kong in summer. People stay indoors. What do people do during a typhoon?
questions.explanation: The text says people stay indoors.

questions.questionText: The Hong Kong Museum of History is free on Wednesdays. When is the museum free?
questions.explanation: It is free on Wednesdays.

questions.questionText: Cantonese is the main language in Hong Kong. What is the main language?
questions.explanation: The passage says Cantonese is the main language.

questions.questionText: Rapid urban development has led to loss of traditional villages. What is implied?

options.D: isappearing C. Development is bad for economy D. No change has happened

questions.explanation: Losing villages implies traditional culture is fading.

questions.questionText: “Not only… but also…” many find it challenging. What is the author’s tone?
questions.explanation: “Many students find challenging” signals concern about difficulty.

questions.questionText: “..., albeit ambitious, ...” What does “albeit” mean?
questions.explanation: Albeit means although.

questions.questionText: “City that never sleeps… suffers from noise pollution at night.” What is the irony?

options.C: ity has noise problems C. No noise at all

questions.explanation: The “never sleeps” image fits noise—but calling it ironically highlights the negative consequence.

questions.questionText: Writer’s purpose in editorial about youth unemployment?

options.B: To urge action from the government
options.D: escribe a festival D. To sell products

questions.explanation: Editorials typically argue/urge action, especially on social issues.

questions.questionText: Flyovers symbolise both progress and congestion. Infer the dual meaning.
questions.explanation: “Both” signals benefit + drawback.

questions.questionText: “Mitigate climate change… include planting more trees.” “Mitigate” means:
questions.explanation: Mitigate = reduce harm/impact.

questions.questionText: Globalisation brings opportunities but erodes local identity. Main concern?
questions.explanation: The concern stated is cultural erosion.

questions.questionText: Tone of article on HKDSE pressure is:
questions.explanation: Focus on pressure suggests criticism of too much stress.

questions.questionText: Proposal overlooks socioeconomic disparities. What does the writer suggest?

options.B: It ignores differences between rich and poor students
options.D: isparities exist D. Students are all the same

questions.explanation: “Overlooks disparities” = ignores rich/poor differences.

questions.questionText: Heritage buildings revitalised—“a testament to shifting priorities.” Infer:

options.C: hanged to protect heritage C. No change in priorities

questions.explanation: Revitalisation shows greater value placed on heritage.

questions.questionText: Paradox: efficient city struggles with work-life balance. What is the paradox?

options.B: alance B. Efficiency exists but balance is poor

questions.explanation: Two ideas seem to conflict: high efficiency yet poor balance.

questions.questionText: Apps enhance learning, but over-reliance may hinder deep thinking. Attitude?
questions.explanation: It recognises benefits but warns about risks = balanced.

questions.questionText: “Underscores the resilience…” “Underscores” means:
questions.explanation: Underscore = emphasise.

questions.questionText: Globalisation intensified competition for limited resources. Implied challenge?
questions.explanation: “Intensified competition” = more competition for limited resources.

questions.questionText: “Notwithstanding… yearn for the simplicity of yesteryear.” What do people yearn for?
questions.explanation: It directly says they yearn for the simpler past.

questions.questionText: Many young people use social media… some worry about too much screen time. What is a common worry?
questions.explanation: The worry mentioned is too much screen time.

questions.questionText: “Public transport is efficient but crowded…” What does “efficient” mean here?
questions.explanation: In transport context, efficient = works well, fast and reliable.

questions.questionText: Reading the newspaper every day helps students learn new words. What is the main idea?

options.B: oring B. Reading newspapers improves vocabulary

questions.explanation: “Learn new words” = improves vocabulary.

questions.questionText: Good English scores open doors to universities. What does a good English score help with?
questions.explanation: It says scores open doors to universities.

questions.questionText: Although the weather is hot, people still enjoy hiking. What can you infer?

options.A: Hiking is impossible in hot weather
options.B: People like hiking even when hot

questions.explanation: They still hike, so they enjoy it despite heat.

questions.questionText: “Hong Kong people are resilient after typhoons.” Meaning of “resilient”?
questions.explanation: Resilient = recover quickly after difficulties.

questions.questionText: Economy depends on tourism and finance. Name two important industries.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly lists tourism and finance.

questions.questionText: Purpose of passage about environmental protection?

options.D: escribe food D. To sell products

questions.explanation: Environmental protection passages commonly aim to persuade action like recycling.

questions.questionText: “MTR is convenient, yet some prefer buses because they see more of the city.” Why choose buses?
questions.explanation: The sentence gives the reason: see more of the city.

questions.questionText: Student practised every day for speaking exam. What helped the student?
questions.explanation: Improvement is linked to daily practice.

questions.questionText: Meaning of “sustainable tourism”?
questions.explanation: Sustainable tourism = tourism that protects the environment.

questions.questionText: “Dim sum restaurants are busy on weekends.” What can you infer?

options.B: Many people eat dim sum on weekends

questions.explanation: If restaurants are busy, many people go then.

questions.questionText: “Vibrant” describes Hong Kong’s night market. It means:
questions.explanation: Vibrant = lively, energetic.

questions.questionText: Main problem about young people in HK?
questions.explanation: The topic focuses on study pressure/stress.

questions.questionText: English is important, but students prefer Cantonese with friends. What does this show?

options.C: omfortable with friends C. No one speaks Cantonese

questions.explanation: Preference shows Cantonese feels more natural socially.

questions.questionText: Reading English books improves writing skills. How does reading help writing?
questions.explanation: The passage directly states reading improves writing.

questions.questionText: Attitude to public holidays: relax but can cause traffic jams.
questions.explanation: It shows both pros and cons, so attitude is mixed.

questions.questionText: Friend: “Would you like to go hiking at Lion Rock this Saturday?”
questions.explanation: It accepts politely and keeps the conversation going by asking for meeting details.

questions.questionText: Teacher: “What do you think about learning English?”
questions.explanation: It gives a clear opinion and supports it with a sensible reason.

questions.questionText: Friend: “Did you enjoy the dim sum lunch yesterday?”

options.B: yes…especially har gow and siu mai

questions.explanation: It answers directly and adds specific details, which makes the reply natural.

questions.questionText: Classmate: “How do you go to school every day?”
questions.explanation: It clearly answers the question and adds a short reason.

questions.questionText: Friend: “The weather is so hot today!”
questions.explanation: It agrees appropriately and uses accurate descriptive language.

questions.questionText: Teacher: “Have you finished your English homework?”
questions.explanation: It is honest and responsible, and it includes a clear plan to complete it.

questions.questionText: Friend: “Do you want to watch a movie at Times Square this weekend?”
questions.explanation: It shows interest and asks a follow-up question to continue planning.

questions.questionText: Stranger: “Excuse me, is this the train to Central?”
questions.explanation: It is polite and gives a complete, helpful answer.

questions.questionText: Mother: “How was your HKDSE mock exam today?”
questions.explanation: It gives a balanced response and provides a specific detail.

questions.questionText: Friend: “Happy Birthday!”
questions.explanation: It shows gratitude in a warm, socially appropriate way.

questions.questionText: Teacher: “Why do you want to improve your English speaking?”
questions.explanation: It gives a clear purpose that is relevant to school and exams.

questions.questionText: Classmate: “Let’s study together for the English exam.”
questions.explanation: It agrees and asks practical questions to arrange the study session.

questions.questionText: Friend: “What is your favourite place in Hong Kong?”
questions.explanation: It answers with a specific place and a reason, which is clear and engaging.

questions.questionText: Waiter: “Would you like some more tea?”
questions.explanation: It is polite and uses appropriate service-language.

questions.questionText: Friend: “I passed my English test!”
questions.explanation: It responds positively and shows support, which fits the situation.

questions.questionText: Teacher: “Can you tell the class about your favourite festival?”
questions.explanation: It gives a complete sentence with a simple reason/example.

questions.questionText: Friend: “Shall we go to Ocean Park during the holidays?”
questions.explanation: It accepts enthusiastically and adds a reason, making it friendly and natural.

questions.questionText: Debate: “HKDSE puts too much pressure on students.”
questions.explanation: It acknowledges the problem but provides a mature, balanced perspective and outcome.

questions.questionText: “Technology made life easier but more complicated.”

options.A: agree…convenience but over-reliance harms deep thinking/communication

questions.explanation: It uses sophisticated language and evaluates both sides with a clear caution.

questions.questionText: “Should HK promote more sustainable tourism?”
questions.explanation: It supports the idea with a well-developed reason that covers environment and economy.

questions.questionText: “Globalisation has more disadvantages than advantages.”
questions.explanation: It gives an effective counter-argument and still admits a drawback, showing nuance.

questions.questionText: “Young people today are too lazy.”
questions.explanation: It avoids stereotyping and offers a balanced explanation supported by context.

questions.questionText: “How can we improve English speaking effectively?”
questions.explanation: It provides multiple concrete methods and uses an organised, formal tone.

questions.questionText: “Social media does more harm than good.”

options.A: risks exist but benefits when used wisely

questions.explanation: It presents a balanced view and shows critical thinking by adding conditions (“used wisely”).

questions.questionText: “Traditional festivals are losing importance.”

options.A: partly true but revitalising preserves identity

questions.explanation: It acknowledges change and proposes a constructive solution linked to cultural identity.

questions.questionText: “Biggest challenge facing HK students today?”
questions.explanation: It synthesises two major pressures into a clear, formal answer.

questions.questionText: “Ban all plastic bags immediately.”
questions.explanation: It supports the goal but evaluates feasibility, offering a practical alternative.

questions.questionText: “Reading books is outdated.”
questions.explanation: It argues respectfully and compares depth/quality of learning, not just preference.

questions.questionText: “Focus more on economic growth than environmental protection.”

options.A: both important; sustainable development supports long-term growth

questions.explanation: It avoids extremes and frames a long-term solution using a formal concept.

questions.questionText: Opinion on AI in education.

options.A: personalise learning but not replace teachers

questions.explanation: It recognises benefits while setting appropriate limits, showing balanced judgment.

questions.questionText: “Living in HK is more difficult for young people.”
questions.explanation: It empathises and gives specific reasons, then adds a balanced hopeful point.

questions.questionText: “More liberal studies or more STEM?”

options.A: balanced curriculum: critical thinking + practical skills

questions.explanation: It gives a well-supported, balanced stance and explains the value of both areas.

questions.questionText: “How do you see your future after HKDSE?”
questions.explanation: B can’t do 6, but proposes 7.

questions.questionText: “Social media is a complete waste of time.”
questions.explanation: It disagrees politely and supports the view with clear reasons.

questions.questionText: “Hong Kong is too crowded. I want to move.”
questions.explanation: It shows empathy while presenting a balanced counterpoint.

questions.questionText: “Should students do more homework?”

options.A: moderate useful but too much stress

questions.explanation: It gives a nuanced opinion by recognising both benefits and drawbacks.

questions.questionText: “The MTR is the best transport.”
questions.explanation: It agrees and provides specific supporting reasons.

questions.questionText: “English is too difficult to learn.”
questions.explanation: It encourages the speaker and offers practical advice.

questions.questionText: “Video games are better than reading books.”

options.A: partly agree but reading improves vocab/imagination

questions.explanation: It responds respectfully and gives a balanced comparison with reasons.

questions.questionText: “Advantages of living in Hong Kong?”
questions.explanation: It answers directly and includes two relevant, concrete advantages.

questions.questionText: “Typhoon signal No.8 is coming. What should we do?”
questions.explanation: It gives sensible safety advice and a clear action plan.

questions.questionText: “Dim sum is the best Hong Kong food.”
questions.explanation: It agrees and extends the idea with an extra meaningful reason.

questions.questionText: “Studying for HKDSE is too stressful.”
questions.explanation: It validates the feeling and suggests helpful coping strategies.

questions.questionText: “Should we use more English in daily life?”
questions.explanation: It gives a clear opinion and links it to a practical benefit.

questions.questionText: “How can we protect the environment in Hong Kong?”
questions.explanation: It provides realistic actions that individuals can do.

questions.questionText: “The Peak is too touristy now.”

options.A: true but view still amazing at night

questions.explanation: It acknowledges the point but adds a positive counterpoint, showing balance.

questions.questionText: “Online learning is better than going to school.”
questions.explanation: It gives a reasonable counter-argument focused on interaction and learning quality.

questions.questionText: “What do you usually do on weekends?”
questions.explanation: It answers with specific details, showing good ability to elaborate.

questions.questionText: “Hong Kong needs more green spaces.”
questions.explanation: It agrees and supports the claim with a clear benefit.

questions.questionText: “One thing you learned from today’s English lesson?”
questions.explanation: It gives a specific learning point with examples, which sounds thoughtful.

questions.questionText: Choose the correct sentence for a formal email.
questions.explanation: “Dear Sir… I am writing to apply…” is formal and polite.

questions.questionText: “I _____ to school by MTR every day.”
questions.explanation: Subject “I” takes base verb go in present simple.

questions.questionText: Correct: “She don’t like English class.”
questions.explanation: Third person singular uses doesn’t.

questions.questionText: Best topic sentence about Hong Kong food.
questions.explanation: It introduces the paragraph topic: HK food.

questions.questionText: “The Peak offers _____ views…”
questions.explanation: Collocation: beautiful views.

questions.questionText: Which is a complete sentence?
questions.explanation: It has a subject + verb and is complete.

questions.questionText: Polite way to ask for help.
questions.explanation: “Could you please…” is polite.

questions.questionText: Correct spelling: “peaple”
questions.explanation: Correct spelling is people.

questions.questionText: Best concluding sentence for studying English.
questions.explanation: A conclusion should sum up positively with a key point.

questions.questionText: Synonym for “happy”: “I feel _____ after passing the test.”
questions.explanation: Joyful means happy.

questions.questionText: “I live _____ Hong Kong Island.”
questions.explanation: For islands, we say on (on Hong Kong Island).

questions.questionText: Best story beginning.
questions.explanation: It’s a complete, natural opening sentence.

questions.questionText: “Students should _____ hard…”
questions.explanation: After “should” use base verb: study.

questions.questionText: Which is formal language?
questions.explanation: “Would you like…” is formal/polite.

questions.questionText: Correct: “There is many tourists…”
questions.explanation: Plural noun “tourists” needs are.

questions.questionText: “I like dim sum _____ it is delicious.”
questions.explanation: “Because” gives the reason.

questions.questionText: Best word: “The view from the Peak is _____.”
questions.explanation: “Breathtaking” is strong positive description for a view.

questions.questionText: Sophisticated language sentence?
questions.explanation: It uses more advanced vocabulary and complex structure.

questions.questionText: Flaw: “In my opinion, I think… you know.”
questions.explanation: “In my opinion/I think” repeats; “you know” is too informal.

questions.questionText: “Brain drain _____ Hong Kong’s talent pool.”
questions.explanation: Brain drain reduces available talent = depletes.

questions.questionText: “Not only does technology aid learning, _____ it can also distract…”
questions.explanation: Pattern is “not only… but also…”

questions.questionText: Error: “Despite of the difficulties…”
questions.explanation: Correct is despite (no “of”) or “in spite of.”

questions.questionText: Best thesis statement: “This essay argues that _____.”
questions.explanation: A thesis must be specific and arguable.

questions.questionText: “The government’s _____ approach…”
questions.explanation: “Proactive approach” is standard academic collocation.

questions.questionText: “Globalisation offers benefits; _____, cultural erosion remains…”
questions.explanation: “However” marks contrast.

questions.questionText: “It is imperative that students _____ English regularly.”
questions.explanation: “Imperative that” calls for an action: practise.

questions.questionText: Counter-argument: “While some claim technology isolates people, _____.”
questions.explanation: It directly rebuts with a plausible opposite point.

questions.questionText: “students whom study hard” correction?
questions.explanation: For subject of the clause, use who (not whom).

questions.questionText: “Ultimately, fostering bilingualism will _____ HK’s competitiveness.”
questions.explanation: Bilingualism would improve/enhance competitiveness.

questions.questionText: “Although progress has been made, much remains…” tone?
questions.explanation: It acknowledges progress but notes more work = balanced.

questions.questionText: “The _____ of urban planning…”
questions.explanation: “Intricacies” means complex details.

questions.questionText: “It is undeniable that _____.”
questions.explanation: It’s a strong persuasive claim appropriate to formal writing.

questions.questionText: Error in formal register: “Kids… mad pressure… innit?”
questions.explanation: Slang (“mad,” “innit”) is not formal.

questions.questionText: “Hong Kong is crowded. _____, it has many parks.”
questions.explanation: “However” shows contrast.

questions.questionText: “The government is _____ new laws…”
questions.explanation: Governments introduce laws.

questions.questionText: Error: “Although the exam was difficult, but I passed it.”
questions.explanation: Don’t use although and but together.

questions.questionText: “In my opinion, _____.”
questions.explanation: It states a clear, appropriate opinion.

questions.questionText: “I woke up late. _____ I missed the bus.”
questions.explanation: “Therefore” shows result.

questions.questionText: “The _____ of the city makes it exciting but stressful.”
questions.explanation: “Fast pace” commonly causes excitement and stress.

questions.questionText: “Hong Kong has excellent public transport. _____”

options.A: The MTR is fast and reliable

questions.explanation: It supports the topic with a relevant example.

questions.questionText: Correct: “Because it was raining, so we stayed home.”

options.C: It was raining so we stayed

questions.explanation: Use because without “so” in the same clause.

questions.questionText: “It is widely believed that _____.”
questions.explanation: It fits an objective, academic tone.

questions.questionText: “The policy aims to _____ social inequality.”
questions.explanation: Policies aim to reduce inequality.

questions.questionText: “Hong Kong is modern. _____, some traditions remain.”
questions.explanation: “Nevertheless” signals contrast.

questions.questionText: “In conclusion, _____.”
questions.explanation: It’s a proper conclusion statement.

questions.questionText: “The typhoon caused significant _____…”
questions.explanation: Typhoons cause damage.

questions.questionText: Which shows good paragraph unity?
questions.explanation: Unity means all sentences stay on one topic.

questions.questionText: “I firmly _____ that…”
questions.explanation: Correct collocation: “firmly believe.”

questions.questionText: Parallel structure error: “Students like reading, to write and studying.”
questions.explanation: To match “reading… studying,” use writing.

questions.questionText: “_____ heavy rain, the match was cancelled.”
questions.explanation: “Because of” + noun phrase shows cause.
